source: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-identity- management#im-7-restrict-resource-access-based-on--conditions 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional- access/concept-conditional-access-users-groups 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/howto- conditional-access-policy-azure-management 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/concept- conditional-access-cloud-apps#windows-azure-service-management-api Additional Information: These policies should be tested by using the What If tool in the References. Setting these can and will create issues with administrators changing settings until they use an MFA device linked to their accounts. An emergency access account is recommended for this eventuality if all administrators are locked out. Please see the documentation in the references for further information. Similarly further testing can also be done via the insights and reporting resource in References which monitors Azure sign ins.

input_row.title: Ensure that multifactor authentication is required for Windows Azure Service Management API
input_row.rationale: This recommendation ensures that users accessing the Windows Azure Service Management API (i.e. Azure Powershell, Azure CLI, Azure Resource Manager API, etc.) are required to use multi-factor authentication (MFA) credentials when accessing resources through the Windows Azure Service Management API.
input_row.audit: Administrative access to the Windows Azure Service Management API should be secured with a higher level of scrutiny to authenticating mechanisms. Enabling multi- factor authentication is recommended to reduce the potential for abuse of Administrative actions, and to prevent intruders or compromised admin credentials from changing administrative settings. IMPORTANT : While this recommendation allows exceptions to specific Users or Groups, they should be very carefully tracked and reviewed for necessity on a regular interval through an Access Review process. It is important that this rule be built to include "All Users" to ensure that all users not specifically excepted will be required to use MFA to access the Azure Service Management API. Impact: Conditional Access policies require Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses. Similarly, they may require additional overhead to maintain if users lose access to their MFA. Any users or groups which are granted an exception to this policy should be carefully tracked, be granted only minimal necessary privileges, and conditional access exceptions should be regularly reviewed or investigated.
input_row.remediation: Audit from Azure Portal 1. From the Azure Admin Portal dashboard, open Microsoft Entra ID. 2. In the menu on the left of the Entra ID blade, click Security. 3. In the menu on the left of the Security blade, click Conditional Access. 4. In the menu on the left of the Conditional Access blade, click Policies. 5. Click on the name of the policy you wish to audit. 6. Click the blue text under Users. 7. Under the Include section of Users, ensure that All Users is selected. 8. Under the Exclude section of Users, review the Users and Groups that are excluded from the policy (NOTE: this should be limited to break-glass emergency access accounts, non-interactive service accounts, and other carefully considered exceptions). 9. On the left side, click the blue text under Target resources. 10. Under the Include section of Target Resources, ensure that the Select apps radio button is selected. 11. Under Select, ensure that Windows Azure Service Management API is listed.
input_row.impact: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. From the Azure Admin Portal dashboard, open Microsoft Entra ID. 2. Click Security in the Entra ID blade. 3. Click Conditional Access in the Security blade. 4. Click Policies in the Conditional Access blade. 5. Click + New policy. 6. Enter a name for the policy. 7. Click the blue text under Users. 8. Under Include, select All users. 9. Under Exclude, check Users and groups. 10. Select users or groups to be exempted from this policy (e.g. break-glass emergency accounts, and non-interactive service accounts) then click the Select button. 11. Click the blue text under Target resources. 12. Under Include, click the Select apps radio button. 13. Click the blue text under Select. 14. Check the box next to Windows Azure Service Management APIs then click the Select button. 15. Click the blue text under Grant. 16. Under Grant access check the box for Require multi-factor authentication then click the Select button. 17. Before creating, set Enable policy to Report-only. 18. Click Create. After testing the policy in report-only mode, update the Enable policy setting from Report-only to On. Default Value: MFA is not enabled by default for administrative actions. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-identity- management#im-7-restrict-resource-access-based-on--conditions 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional- access/concept-conditional-access-users-groups 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/howto- conditional-access-policy-azure-management 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/concept- conditional-access-cloud-apps#windows-azure-service-management-api Additional Information: These policies should be tested by using the What If tool in the References. Setting these can and will create issues with administrators changing settings until they use an MFA device linked to their accounts. An emergency access account is recommended for this eventuality if all administrators are locked out. Please see the documentation in the references for further information. Similarly further testing can also be done via the insights and reporting resource in References which monitors Azure sign ins.

Any users or groups which are granted an exception to this policy should be carefully tracked, be granted only minimal necessary privileges, and conditional access exceptions should be regularly reviewed or investigated.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control can be validated through API queries regarding Conditional Access policies. The requirements for users to have multi-factor authentication enabled for specific APIs can be accessed using Azure AD APIs, as the structure and settings of Conditional Access are exposed via Microsoft Graph API.
  
**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_ad_conditional_access_mfa_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program would check the following:
1. Utilize the Microsoft Graph API to query the Conditional Access policies configured in the Azure Active Directory.
2. Resource to enumerate: All Conditional Access policies related to the Azure Service Management APIs.
3. Specific properties to validate:
   - Ensure there's a Conditional Access policy labeled as requiring multi-factor authentication.
   - Validate that "All Users" is included in the targeted user assignment.
   - Confirm that Windows Azure Service Management API is listed under the resources targeted for the policy.
4. Pass/Fail Criteria:
   - Pass: A policy exists that requires MFA for "All Users" targeting the Windows Azure Service Management API.
   - Fail: No such policy exists or deviations in the user assignments/exceptions as per compliance requirements.
5. Example API Endpoints:
   - To list Conditional Access policies: `GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/conditionalAccess/policies`
   - To check policy assignments: Check the `conditions` section of each policy for correct user targeting and permissions. 

This automated approach ensures compliance with the control that requires MFA for administrative access via the Azure Service Management API.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as Automated is mostly correct, as the Microsoft Graph API can be used to query Conditional Access policies, which allows for automation. However, manual verification might still be necessary to ensure the policies are correctly interpreted and applied, especially in complex environments.
- **Azure APIs/CLI Verification:** This can indeed be checked via Azure APIs, specifically the Microsoft Graph API. Additionally, Azure CLI and PowerShell cmdlets can also be used to query and validate Conditional Access policies.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- **Automation Details:** The automation details are mostly correct. The use of the Microsoft Graph API to query Conditional Access policies is appropriate, and the example API endpoint provided is accurate. However, the assessment could benefit from more detailed instructions on how to interpret the `conditions` section of each policy.
- **Manual Steps:** The manual steps are not explicitly outlined in the original assessment, which could be a limitation if the automated checks need to be supplemented by manual verification.
- **Program Name Convention:** The program name follows a logical convention, but it could be more descriptive. A suggestion might be `azure_ad_conditional_access_mfa_service_management`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Detailed Interpretation:** Provide more detailed guidance on interpreting the `conditions` section of the policies, especially regarding user assignments and exceptions.
- **Error Handling:** Consider adding error handling and logging mechanisms in the automated program to capture any issues during API queries.
- **Emergency Access Account:** While mentioned, the setup and testing of an emergency access account should be more thoroughly integrated into the audit process to ensure administrators are not locked out.
- **Additional Tools:** Mention the use of Azure Monitor and Azure Security Center for additional insights and reporting on Conditional Access policy effectiveness.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The use of Microsoft Graph API for querying Conditional Access policies is a well-established method, and the overall approach is sound. However, the need for manual verification in complex scenarios should not be overlooked.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** While the automated approach is largely valid, it should be supplemented with manual verification steps to ensure comprehensive compliance. Additionally, improve the program name for clarity and include more detailed instructions on interpreting policy conditions. Consider integrating emergency access account setup and testing into the audit process.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The compliance control can be effectively audited using the Microsoft Graph API, which allows querying of Conditional Access policies in Azure Active Directory. This capability supports the automated verification of multi-factor authentication requirements for the Windows Azure Service Management API. Both AI opinions acknowledge the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion suggesting partial manual verification, which is not necessary given the comprehensive API capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_ad_conditional_access_mfa_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** Azure Active Directory
- **API/CLI Method:** Microsoft Graph API
- **Properties to Check:** 
  - Conditional Access policies targeting the Windows Azure Service Management API
  - Ensure "All Users" is included in the policy
  - Verify that multi-factor authentication is required
- **Pass Criteria:** 
  - A policy exists that requires MFA for all users accessing the Windows Azure Service Management API
- **Fail Criteria:** 
  - Absence of such a policy or exclusion of users from MFA requirement

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The second AI opinion suggested partial manual verification due to potential complexity, but given the robust capabilities of the Microsoft Graph API, full automation is feasible and preferable for consistent and repeatable compliance checks.
